how do i know if we have product-market.problem: How do I know if we have product-market fit?

solutions.speaker: Sean Ellis
solutions.insight: Product-market fit is when your customers love what you built so much they tell their friends. If you're not growing, you haven't found it yet.

solutions.speaker: Brian Balfour
solutions.insight: Thoughtful approach to how do i know if we have product-market fit? is key. Understanding your audience and iterating based on feedback helps you make better decisions.

solutions.speaker: Marty Cagan
solutions.insight: Focus on the outcomes you want to achieve, not just shipping features. The best teams start with the customer problem and work backwards.

how do i prioritize what to build next?.problem: How do I prioritize what to build next?

solutions.speaker: Marty Cagan
solutions.insight: Focus on the outcomes you want to achieve, not just shipping features. The best teams start with the customer problem and work backwards.

solutions.speaker: Richard Rumelt
solutions.speaker_role: of people out there willing to sell you advice on mission and your vision and your values,
solutions.insight: Good strategy is about focusing on what truly matters. You can't do everything, so be clear about your constraints and priorities.

solutions.speaker: Jake Knapp + John Zeratsky
solutions.insight: Use the sprint methodology to make faster decisions. When you're faced with too many options, timeboxing forces you to make choices.

how do we launch and market a new produc.problem: How do we launch and market a new product?

solutions.speaker: Jason M Lemkin
solutions.insight: Go-to-market strategy is everything. Your product won't sell itself—you need a clear customer acquisition and retention strategy.

solutions.speaker: April Dunford
solutions.insight: Positioning is about how customers perceive your product relative to alternatives. Get this right and everything else becomes easier.

solutions.speaker: Andy Raskin_
solutions.insight: The best way to communicate is to tell stories that resonate with your audience. Facts tell, but stories sell.
